query: effect of dexamethasone on intraocular pressure

retrieved_docs.title: Methylprednisolone -- Adverse Effects -- Ophthalmology:
retrieved_docs.content: Cataracts Increased intraocular pressure Exophthalmos

retrieved_docs.title: Dexamethasone -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Serum electrolytes levels, blood pressure Serum glucose levels, HbA1c [20] Periodic eye examination (long term dexamethasone increases the risk of cataracts and glaucoma) [21] Bone mineral density scans [22] Creatine kinase levels (long term use of glucocorticoids such as dexamethasone predisposes to myopathy; weakness is primarily proximal) [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Adverse Effects -- Ophthalmologic Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: The risk of cataracts is significantly high in patients taking prednisone more than 10 mg daily for more than one year, with a dose-dependence in a linear fashion. However, an increased risk of cataracts has been reported even with low-dose glucocorticoids. [14] Cataracts are usually bilateral and slowly progressing. [15] Increased intraocular pressure, especially in patients with a family history of open-angle glaucoma, is seen in patients receiving intraocular glucocorticoids and high dose systemic glucocorticoids. [16] Glaucoma is often painless and can lead to visual field loss, optic disc cupping, and optic nerve atrophy. After discontinuing systemic therapy, the elevation in intraocular pressure usually resolves within a few weeks, but the damage to the optic nerve is often permanent. A rare adverse effect of systemic or even topical use of glucocorticoids is central serous chorioretinopathy; this leads to the formation of subretinal fluid in the macular region, which leads to separation of the retina from its underlying photoreceptors. This condition manifests as central visual blur and reduced visual acuity. [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Monitoring -- Assessment of Ophthalmological Complications
retrieved_docs.content: An annual ophthalmological examination shall be considered, especially for those with symptoms of cataracts, and early referral for intraocular pressure assessment should occur if there is a personal or family history of open-angle glaucoma, diabetes mellitus, or high myopia.

retrieved_docs.title: Dexamethasone -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Although dexamethasone is generally well tolerated, it does have its drawbacks as a medication. The most frequently reported adverse effect by patients is the presence of insomnia after use. Other frequent adverse effects include acne, indigestion, fluid retention, electrolyte imbalances, weight gain, increased appetite, anorexia, nausea, vomiting, acne, agitation, and depression. There have been reports of adrenal suppression, arrhythmias, spermatogenic changes, glaucoma, hypokalemia, pulmonary edema, pseudotumor cerebri, and increased intracranial pressure. [14] Steroid-induced osteonecrosis of the femoral head(long-term treatment). [15] Hepatotoxicity: Likelihood score: A (well-established cause of liver injury when given in high doses, due to reactivation of hepatitis-B or a hepatocellular injury after high dose treatment. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Sympathetic Ophthalmia -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management -- Corticosteroid-Induced Side Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoporosis prevention : Calcium, vitamin D supplements, and bone density monitoring Hyperglycemia control: Blood sugar level monitoring and collaborating with a primary care physician for diabetes management Increased infection risk: Educate patients on signs of infection; possibly prescribe prophylactic antibiotics for specific situations Cataract and glaucoma: Regular eye examinations, pressure monitoring, and surgeries as needed

retrieved_docs.title: Herpes Zoster Ophthalmicus -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management -- Topical Corticosteroids (Prednisolone Acetate, Dexamethasone)
retrieved_docs.content: Common adverse effects: Elevated intraocular pressure → steroid-induced glaucoma. Cataract formation with prolonged use. Worsening of epithelial keratitis or risk of secondary infection. Management: Regularly monitor IOP during therapy. Use the lowest effective dose with gradual tapering. Avoid unsupervised long-term use. Add prophylactic lubricants to minimize epithelial compromise. [72]

retrieved_docs.title: Prednisone -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Glaucoma: Corticosteroid therapy lasting longer than a few weeks may raise ocular pressure. Patients receiving chronic treatment should get routine eye checks to catch changes early. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management -- Corticosteroid-Related Toxicities
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids are often used off-label in selected early cases of NAION. However, their use requires careful consideration due to potential adverse effects. Adverse Effects: Hyperglycemia Hypertension Immunosuppression Mood changes Steroid-induced glaucoma or cataract (ocular) Management: Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest time Monitor blood glucose and blood pressure Coordinate care with endocrinology in patients with diabetes mellitus Avoid use in patients with uncontrolled systemic conditions [100]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Hypotony -- Treatment / Management -- Medical/Conservative Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Options for treatment include systemic corticosteroids, systemic nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, a topical steroid (including prednisolone acetate, difluprednate) and non-steroid, and local injection of steroids (including posterior subtenon triamcinolone, peribulbar/transseptal triamcinolone/methylprednisolone). In some cases, systemic immunomodulatory agents could be used to manage the primary inflammatory condition. Corticosteroid therapy can be used with good effect if the cause of hypotony is associated with intraocular inflammation and shuts down the production of aqueous by the ciliary processes. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Neuritis -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: Cataracts and glaucoma: Chronic corticosteroid use can lead to cataract formation or steroid-induced glaucoma. Regular ophthalmologic examinations, including intraocular pressure monitoring, should be performed if prolonged steroid use is anticipated. [86]

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Monitoring -- Baseline Assessment and Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Preexisting conditions that should be assessed for and treated when starting glucocorticoids include: Diabetes mellitus Poorly controlled hypertension Heart failure and peripheral edema Cataract or glaucoma Peptic ulcer disease Presence of infection Low bone density or osteoporosis Psychiatric illness

retrieved_docs.title: Iritis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Sustained-release corticosteroid vitreous implants (fluocinolone acetonide, dexamethasone) are available to treat inflammation-induced cases of panuveitis, intermediate uveitis, and posterior uveitis. Corticosteroids should be initiated only in conjunction with the approval of an ophthalmologist because uveitis is a diagnosis of exclusion. Steroids can have adverse effects, such as causing intraocular pressure, cataract formation, steroid-induced glaucoma, and the development of herpes keratitis. Potassium-sparing drugs should be used when chronic steroid use is required to control inflammation. Approximately half of the patients with uveitis need treatment beyond corticosteroid treatment to prevent vision loss. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Neuritis -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid toxicity: Intravenous methylprednisolone carries the typical risks associated with steroid therapy, such as hyperglycemia, hypertension, insomnia, mood changes, and gastrointestinal upset. Blood glucose and blood pressure should be monitored to manage these adverse reactions, particularly in patients with preexisting conditions. Proton pump inhibitors or H2 blockers may be prescribed to prevent gastric irritation, and patients should be advised on proper sleep hygiene and monitored for psychiatric symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Steroid-Induced Glaucoma -- Pathophysiology -- Cortisone Treatment Route
retrieved_docs.content: Studies have shown that about 75% of patients with fluocinolone acetonide implants required IOP lowering medication, and one-third required glaucoma surgery. [28] In comparison, patients with dexamethasone implants with OHT and SID were reported to be much less, with 26.5% and up to 3.2% requiring local drops and surgery, respectively. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Lens-Induced Inflammation -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In lens-induced uveitis after trauma, calculating the power of the intraocular lens (IOL) may be challenging. In an eye with a disfigured cornea, the keratometry of the other eye may have to be used for the calculation. [42] Axial length measurement of the eye with trauma if the globe is intact (blunt trauma or repaired corneoscleral perforation) can be done to calculate intraocular lens power. In the case of the eye with phacolytic glaucoma or phacogenic uveitis, biometry of the same eye can be done. Preoperatively, reduction of intraocular pressure should be tried with intravenous mannitol, oral acetazolamide, and topical glaucoma medications. Topical steroids help quieten the eye and may reduce postoperative ocular inflammation in an advanced cataract with lens-induced inflammation. The formation of arachidonic acid is blocked by corticosteroids, thus preventing the release of inflammatory mediators such as prostaglandins, leukotrienes, and thromboxane. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2596
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class “corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, ophthalmologic and otologic” comprises specific drug combinations used for eye and ear infections, including “dexamethasone and antiinfectives” and “prednisolone and antiinfectives.” Both dexamethasone‑based and prednisolone‑based anti‑infective regimens are members of this combined corticosteroid‑antibiotic category, reflecting their shared role in treating ophthalmic and otologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2294
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFLAMMATORY AGENTS AND ANTIINFECTIVES IN COMBINATION represent a therapeutic class that pairs anti‑inflammatory and antimicrobial actions. Within this broad class, the specific subgroup Corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, opthalmic comprises eye‑drop formulations that combine a corticosteroid with an anti‑infective agent to treat ocular inflammation and infection concurrently. The combination dexamethasone and antiinfectives is a member of this ophthalmic corticosteroid‑anti‑infective subgroup, illustrating how dexamethasone, a potent anti‑inflammatory corticosteroid, is paired with antimicrobial agents to provide dual therapeutic effects in eye care. This integrated approach supports effective management of ocular conditions where both inflammation and infection are present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, ophthalmologic, represents a therapeutic class of eye‑drop formulations that pair a corticosteroid with an antimicrobial agent. Specific members of this class include hydrocortisone and antiinfectives as well as prednisolone and antiinfectives. By combining the anti‑inflammatory properties of corticosteroids such as hydrocortisone or prednisolone with the infection‑controlling effects of antiinfective agents, these ophthalmic preparations provide dual action for the treatment of inflammatory ocular conditions that are complicated by bacterial or fungal infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2760
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic category “corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, ophthalmologic and otologic” comprises combination products that pair a corticosteroid with an anti‑infective agent for use in eye and ear conditions. Within this class, both “fludrocortisone and antiinfectives” and “prednisolone and antiinfectives” are recognized members. These specific combinations integrate the anti‑inflammatory effects of fludrocortisone or prednisolone with antimicrobial activity, providing clinicians with integrated treatment options for ophthalmic and otologic infections where simultaneous control of inflammation and infection is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, ophthalmologic” comprises specific therapeutic pairings used for eye conditions, including the combination of dexamethasone with antiinfective agents and the combination of fludrocortisone with antiinfective agents. Both dexamethasone and fludrocortisone, when paired with antiinfectives, are considered members of this ophthalmologic corticosteroid‑antibiotic combination class, reflecting their role in integrated ocular treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1593
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, opthalmologic and corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, otologic are formulation categories that pair a corticosteroid with an anti‑infective agent for use in the eye and ear, respectively. Both categories include the specific combination products hydrocortisone and antiinfectives, dexamethasone and antiinfectives, fluocinolone acetonide and antiinfectives, and prednisolone and antiinfectives. By integrating the anti‑inflammatory effects of the corticosteroids (hydrocortisone, dexamethasone, fluocinolone acetonide, prednisolone) with the antimicrobial activity of the antiinfectives, these combination preparations are designed to treat ocular or otic infections while simultaneously reducing inflammation, thereby improving therapeutic outcomes and patient comfort.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1518
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, ophthalmic and otologic, is a therapeutic formulation that includes hydrocortisone and antiinfective agents, providing both anti‑inflammatory and antimicrobial effects for eye and ear conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2722
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, ophthalmologic is a therapeutic class that includes the specific combination products hydrocortisone and antiinfectives and dexamethasone and antiinfectives. These ophthalmic formulations pair a corticosteroid (hydrocortisone or dexamethasone) with an antiinfective agent to provide simultaneous anti‑inflammatory and antimicrobial activity in eye care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: target organ (ear or eye), by formulation type (single‑organ or combined ophthalmic‑otologic), and by specific active steroid component (betamethasone, hydrocortisone, dexamethasone, fludrocortisone, fluocinolone acetonide, prednisolone). This structured taxonomy facilitates precise identification and appropriate therapeutic selection for managing infections and inflammatory conditions of the ear and eye.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 103
retrieved_docs.content: drug classes, combination strategies (corticosteroid + anti‑infective ± mydriatic or non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory), dosage‑form specifications, and therapeutic as well as diagnostic applications, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care for eye and ear conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1891
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethasone and antiinfectives, hydrocortisone and antiinfectives, dexamethasone and antiinfectives, and fludrocortisone and antiinfectives are each classified as members of the group “Corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, opthalmologic.” The same four specific corticosteroid‑antibiotic combinations are also designated as members of the broader group “corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, opthalmologic and otologic.” This dual categorization reflects that these formulations are intended for use in ophthalmic conditions and, more generally, in both ophthalmic and otologic infections, combining the anti‑inflammatory effects of the corticosteroids with the antimicrobial activity of the antiinfective agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2728
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, opthalmologic is a therapeutic category that encompasses specific ophthalmic formulations in which a corticosteroid is paired with an antiinfective agent. Both dexamethasone and antiinfectives and fluocinolone acetonide and antiinfectives are members of this category, reflecting the use of these combined agents to provide simultaneous anti‑inflammatory and antimicrobial effects in eye care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1515
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, ophthalmologic and otologic, is a therapeutic category that incorporates dexamethasone and antiinfectives, fludrocortisone and antiinfectives, and prednisolone and antiinfectives. A related category, corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, otologic, likewise includes dexamethasone and antiinfectives, fludrocortisone and antiinfectives, and prednisolone and antiinfectives. These combination formulations pair the anti‑inflammatory actions of the corticosteroids dexamethasone, fludrocortisone, and prednisolone with antimicrobial agents to treat infections and inflammatory conditions of the eye and ear, providing a coordinated approach to ophthalmologic and otologic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2759
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, otologic is a therapeutic class that includes the specific formulations hydrocortisone and antiinfectives as well as dexamethasone and antiinfectives. Both hydrocortisone‑based and dexamethasone‑based combinations pair a corticosteroid with an antimicrobial agent to treat otologic conditions, leveraging the anti‑inflammatory properties of the steroid together with the infection‑controlling effects of the antiinfective.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1523
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid–antiinfective combination products are formulated for both ophthalmic and otologic applications. In the ophthalmic category, the combinations include hydrocortisone and antiinfectives, dexamethasone and antiinfectives, fludrocortisone and antiinfectives, and fluocinolone acetonide and antiinfectives. The same four corticosteroid–antiinfective pairings—hydrocortisone and antiinfectives, dexamethasone and antiinfectives, fludrocortisone and antiinfectives, and fluocinolone acetonide and antiinfectives—are also classified within the otologic category. These combination therapies integrate the anti‑inflammatory effects of corticosteroids with antimicrobial activity, providing targeted treatment for infections and inflammatory conditions of the eye and ear.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2787
retrieved_docs.content: The ophthalmic preparation category “Corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, ophthalmologic” comprises specific combination products that pair a corticosteroid with an anti‑infective agent. Within this category, formulations that combine fludrocortisone with an anti‑infective are included, as are formulations that combine prednisolone with an anti‑infective. Thus, both “fludrocortisone and antiinfectives” and “prednisolone and antiinfectives” are recognized as members of the broader class of corticosteroid‑antiinfective ophthalmic combinations, reflecting their shared therapeutic purpose of delivering concurrent anti‑inflammatory and antimicrobial effects in eye care.
